method2.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a shallow submarine stratovolcano in the southwest Pacific Ocean, about 24 km south of Vangunu Island in the western Solomon Islands.[2] It lies on an active convergent margin where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate, within the broader Pacific Ring of Fire.[2][3] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, with its summit lying about 20 m below sea level; other descriptions characterize it as a roughly conical seamount rising from about 1,100 m depth and measuring about 8 km across at its base.[2][4] Its summit area contains an oblong, pockmarked crater measuring approximately 120 m by 75 m, with a rim at an average depth of 24 m and deepest soundings on the main peak of about 70 m below sea level.[5] The flanks of the main peak descend at roughly 18° to depths greater than 1,000 m.[5] Bathymetric surveys have also identified a secondary “southwest extension” or summit about 1.3 km southwest of the main summit, rising to 260 m below sea level and separated from the main peak by a narrow col.[5] Because the summit is very shallow, eruptions can discolour surface waters and produce plumes visible from aircraft or satellites.[2] Repeated eruptive activity has occasionally built short-lived islands above sea level, which are later eroded or submerged by wave action and renewed volcanic activity.[2][4][5]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi is among the most active submarine volcanoes in the Pacific, with near-surface eruptions every few years that can build temporary islands.[1] Its first recorded eruption was in 1939, and at least nine new islands have formed during eruptions since then.[1] These islands have generally been too small and insufficiently protected by solid lava flows to withstand wave erosion, and have usually been submerged again within months.[1] In late 1951, frequent small tsunamis were reported in association with activity at Kavachi.[1]

In May 2000, after about nine years of dormancy, scientists aboard the CSIRO research vessel Franklin observed a new phase of island-building activity, with ash and incandescent lava blocks ejected up to 70 m above sea level and sulfurous steam plumes rising to 500 m.[2] Explosive eruptions occurred daily from 27 November to 13 December 2001, and on 13 January 2002 volcanic debris and gas bubbles were observed rising from the submarine vent amid frequent loud noises.[2] During an eruptive cycle in 2003, a 15 m-high island formed, but by 16 November the summit had subsided or eroded to about 32 m below sea level and no volcanic activity was observed for the first time in four years.[2] Volcanic activity was observed again on 15 March 2004 after a long period of quiescence.[2]

A January 2015 expedition reached Kavachi during a rare lull in eruptive activity, allowing researchers to access the active crater and flanks, which are normally difficult to study because of the volcano’s explosive behavior.[3] The 2015 observations documented hydrothermal activity, including warm water and carbon dioxide venting from the crater, as well as diffuse-flow venting from a secondary peak southwest of the main summit.[3][4] The Global Volcanism Project has counted 33 historic eruptions at Kavachi, with activity spanning 44 of 87 years since 1939, and the eruption that began in 2021 was reported as continuing as of May 2026.[1]

==Marine life==
Marine life has been observed in Kavachi’s crater during quiescent periods, despite the volcano’s acidic, mineral-rich and thermally altered waters.[2] Expeditions have recorded an ecosystem that includes gelatinous zooplankton, reef fish, microbial communities and sharks inside or near the crater.[4][5] Shark species reported from Kavachi include the scalloped hammerhead shark and the silky shark, which were filmed swimming in the crater during the Phillips et al. study.[4] The volcano’s association with sharks has led to the popular nickname “Sharkcano” or “Sharkano”.[1][2]

Environmental conditions in the crater are considered extreme because eruptions and hydrothermal activity release heat, suspended particles, carbon dioxide, sulfur-rich gases and other volcanic material into the water.[4][5] Measurements reported from Kavachi include elevated temperatures above surrounding seawater and reduced pH, making the crater an important site for studying marine tolerance to heat and acidity.[1][4] Microbial populations, including sulfur-using bacteria such as members of the genus Sulfurimonas, may exploit volcanic sulfur and carbon dioxide through chemosynthesis.[4] Scientists remain uncertain whether larger animals such as sharks live in the crater continuously or enter it only during calmer eruptive phases.[1][5] Proposed explanations for their presence include behavioral avoidance of eruptions, sensitivity to chemical or seismic warning signs, and physiological tolerance of acidic or heated water.[1] Kavachi is therefore regarded as a natural laboratory for studying extreme marine ecosystems, biological adaptation and the effects of changing ocean chemistry.[1][5]
